learning_outcomes.description: Identify and explain key counter-terrorism laws and approaches.
Identify and explain basic human rights principles and the mechanisms by which human rights are protected.
Identify and discuss key legal and political processes and challenges in the field of terrorism law and human rights.
Conduct and communicate research in terrorism and human rights, applying appropriate research principles and methods.
Investigate and critically analyse complex information, problems and concepts in relation to terrorism and human rights.
Independently critique and evaluate counter-terrorism law, practice and policy, including from a human rights perspective.

assessments.due_date:
17/09/2026 2:00 pm

Exam will occur during scheduled class time in Week 8.
assessments.task_description: An invigilated handwritten exam will be held in class in Week 8. Further information, including examinable topics, will be available on Blackboard and discussed in class.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

assessments.due_date:
24/08/2026 - 23/10/2026

This assessment is due in-class at the beginning of the Seminar. Exercises will be set for each of the Seminars that has an assigned 'Topic'. Further information - and the exercises themselves - will be available on Blackboard.
assessments.category: Paper/ Report/ Annotation, Reflection, Tutorial/ Problem Set
assessments.task_description: Students may hand in 5 exercises during the Semester for 2-marks each (total = 10 marks). There will be 7 opportunities to hand in an exercise, but only 5 will be counted. Exercises must be handed in to the teacher in-person (by the student) on paper at the beginning of the allocated class. Exercises will not be accepted by email. There is no option to defer this assessment or get an extension. Exercises may be hand-written or typed. Each exercise instructions will provide advice on word length and/or formatting.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Hard-copy assignment handed in to the teacher by the student at the beginning of the relevant seminar.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.due_date:
22/10/2026

Presentations will take place in scheduled class time in Week 12 (unless otherwise notified on Blackboard and/or in class). Final scheduling will depend on class enrollment.
assessments.task_description: Students will make an in-person presentation on their research essay topic in Week 12 (the week before the research essay is due). Presentations will be scheduled during class time - any changes to that approach will be detailed on Blackboard and discussed in class. Marks will be allocated for both the presentation and for responses to questions following the presentation. Further details regarding presentation length, schedule and marking criteria will also be provided on Blackboard and discussed in class and a dedicated lecture recording.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description: The primary piece of assessment for this course is a research essay involving independent and well-researched legal and scholarly analysis in the field of counter-terrorism and human rights. Students will choose from a list of essay topics. The presentation assessment will relate directly to the chosen essay topic and the student's research and writing progress.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Assignments must be submitted electronically in Word format via the submission link on the course Blackboard site. Please pay close attention to late penalties and to due dates and times. Late submission will be penalised. If you are not sure whether the online submission portal worked, then email the coordinator directly with your assignment BEFORE the due date and time.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeksFrom Week 4 To Week 12(17 Aug - 25 Oct)
learning_activities.topic:
Seminars

This course will cover the following topics:

  • Week 4 - Topic 1. What is Terrorism?
  • Week 5 - No seminar
  • Week 6 - Topic 2. Using Law to Identify and Criminalise Terrorism and Terrorists
  • Week 7 - Topic 3. Processes of (Secret) Justice: From Surveillance to Evidence
  • Week 8 - In Class Exam
  • Week 9 - Topic 4. Preventive Justice: Control Orders, Preventive Detention, and Post-Sentence Imprisonment
  • Week 10 - Topic 5. Controlling Global Movement for Security: Foreign Fighters, Citizenship and Migration
  • Week 11 - Topic 6. Crimes of Communication: Espionage and Secrecy -vs- Free Speech and Journalism
  • Week 12 - Topic 7. (Lecture) Research, Writing and Careers in National Security; (Seminars) Research Presentations

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06
